title: The Henry Ford Collections and Research
text:
# Digital Collections

The Henry Ford Archive of American Innovation houses 26 million artifacts, 100,000+ digitized, highlighting American ingenuity.

Search The Collection

Search The Collection

## Latest Articles in Collections

[Hosting a Movement: Hospitality in the Jackson Home - The Jackson Home was known as “The House by the Side of the Road” for the way it welcomed everyone who came by. It was this welcoming spirit that would place the home at the center of history, as the struggle for Civil Rights came to Selma in 1965.](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/hosting-a-movement-hospitality-in-the-jackson-home)

Article

#### Hosting a Movement: Hospitality in the Jackson Home

The Jackson Home was known as “The House by the Side of the Road” for the way it welcomed everyone who came by. It was this welcoming spirit that would place the home at the center of history, as the struggle for Civil Rights came to Selma in 1965.

[The Midnight Ride of Paul Revere: The Battle of Lexington and Concord - “LISTEN, my children, and you shall hear Of the midnight ride of Paul Revere, On the eighteenth of April, in Seventy-five; Hardly a man is now alive Who remembers that famous day and year.”](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/2026/the-midnight-ride-of-paul-revere--the-battle-of-lexington-and-concord)

Article

#### The Midnight Ride of Paul Revere: The Battle of Lexington and Concord

“LISTEN, my children, and you shall hear Of the midnight ride of Paul Revere, On the eighteenth of April, in Seventy-five; Hardly a man is now alive Who remembers that famous day and year.”

[From Sketch to Sky: How Leonardo da Vinci Thought Like an Inventor - Leonardo da Vinci, born in the 15th century, described and drew many ideas in his notebooks that bear a remarkable resemblance to real inventions of the 20th century.](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/2026/from-sketch-to-sky--how-leonardo-da-vinci-thought-like-an-inventor)

Article

#### From Sketch to Sky: How Leonardo da Vinci Thought Like an Inventor

Leonardo da Vinci, born in the 15th century, described and drew many ideas in his notebooks that bear a remarkable resemblance to real inventions of the 20th century.

[Lillian Schwartz & the Mona Leo Theory - In the mid-1980s, the multi-hyphenate artist and filmmaker Lillian Schwartz began to promote the computer as a tool capable of solving age-old mysteries in art history, architecture, and archeology.](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/lillian-schwartz-the-mona-leo-theory)

Article

#### Lillian Schwartz & the Mona Leo Theory

In the mid-1980s, the multi-hyphenate artist and filmmaker Lillian Schwartz began to promote the computer as a tool capable of solving age-old mysteries in art history, architecture, and archeology.

[American Women, American Craft - A new exhibition in Henry Ford Museum of American Innovation’s Collections Gallery – Handmade: The Crafting of America – opens to the public on March 21, 2026. This exhibit features stories from the rich 250 years of craft in America. As March is Women’s History Month, here are some highlights of the contributions of women in craft in our collections and in the exhibition.](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/american-women-american-craft)

Article

#### American Women, American Craft

A new exhibition in Henry Ford Museum of American Innovation’s Collections Gallery – Handmade: The Crafting of America – opens to the public on March 21, 2026. This exhibit features stories from the rich 250 years of craft in America. As March is Women’s History Month, here are some highlights of the contributions of women in craft in our collections and in the exhibition.

[“Raw Courage”: Farm Families from Marion to Montgomery, 1965 - Farm families in Alabama knew well the association between the countryside and lawlessness. Actions during February and March 1965 indicated the raw courage required within and beyond the countryside to effect change.](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/raw-courage-farm-families-from-marion-to-montgomery-1965)

Article

#### “Raw Courage”: Farm Families from Marion to Montgomery, 1965

Farm families in Alabama knew well the association between the countryside and lawlessness. Actions during February and March 1965 indicated the raw courage required within and beyond the countryside to effect change.

[Remembering Melvin Parson (1964-2026) - Melvin Parson enriched our lives through his commitment to helping formerly incarcerated individuals plant new futures. He knew of the value of this work because of his own experiences. Starting with a small garden plot in his aunt’s yard, he built an organization that supported individuals and delivered inspiration.](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/remembering-melvin-parson-1964-2026)

Article

#### Remembering Melvin Parson (1964-2026)

Melvin Parson enriched our lives through his commitment to helping formerly incarcerated individuals plant new futures. He knew of the value of this work because of his own experiences. Starting with a small garden plot in his aunt’s yard, he built an organization that supported individuals and delivered inspiration.

[Dyeing With Nature–Earth’s Color Palette - The Living History programs at Greenfield Village give us a step back in time to see how products were made, food was grown, and life was lived before technological production took "making" out of the fabric of everyday life.](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/dyeing-with-nature-earths-color-palette)

Article

#### Dyeing With Nature–Earth’s Color Palette

The Living History programs at Greenfield Village give us a step back in time to see how products were made, food was grown, and life was lived before technological production took "making" out of the fabric of everyday life.

## Curated Sets

View All Sets

Curated sets

#### Jackson Home: The House by the Side of the Road

* 18 Artifacts

The Jackson Home, originally located in Selma, Alabama, provided refuge and solace for Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and others who worked, collaborated, strategized, and planned the Selma-to-Montgomery marches of 1965. The marches led to the signing of the Voting Rights Act on August 6, 1965, enshrining voting equality for all Americans as law.

Read More

Curated sets

#### Influencing Fashion

* 14 Artifacts

Magazines and catalogs have encouraged a desire for fashion since the late 19th century. Serving as style guides, they spread news of trends to those eager to keep up with the latest looks. Though high fashion might be out of reach for some, looking stylish is not.

Read More

## Explore by Theme

Explore The Henry Ford's Collection through a broad array of artifacts and extraordinary stories.

* ### Agriculture & the Environment
* ### Communications & Information Technology
* ### Design & Making
* ### Mobility
* ### Social Transformation
* ### Power & Energy

Farming required year‑round labor and specialized skills, prompting farm families to use tools that reduced physical strain. The shift from animal to mechanical power transformed farm life and raised questions about sustainability, best understood through historic farming technologies.

[Learn More](https://thf-prod-nextjs.enqbator.com/collections/agriculture-and-the-environment)

## Recently Added

Artifact

#### Secretary Desk Used by Martin Luther King, Jr. in the Jackson Home, Selma, Alabama

This desk sat in the Selma, Alabama, home of Dr. Sullivan and Richie Jean Sherrod Jackson, friends of Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. During the struggle to win voting rights in the 1960s, King often stayed with the Jacksons. At this desk, King outlined speeches that he would give -- first, composing his thoughts on paper, then committing them to memory.

Article

#### The Jackson Home: Furnishing It Like It’s 1965

In this interview, Jeanine Head Miller (Curator of Domestic Life), and Charles Sable (Curator of Decorative Arts) sat down with Kristen Gallerneaux (Curator and Editor-in-Chief of Digital Curation) to share their efforts to restore the interiors and furnishings of The Jackson Home. This article is part of an ongoing series focusing on the history, preservation, and restoration of the landmark Jackson Home experience, slated to open in Greenfield Village in Summer 2026.

[](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/the-jackson-home-furnishing-it-like-its-1965)

Artifact

#### Armchair Used by Martin Luther King, Jr. in the Jackson Home, Selma, Alabama

On March 15, 1965, Rev. Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and several of his lieutenants gathered in the Selma, Alabama, home of Dr. Sullivan and Richie Jean Sherrod Jackson to watch President Lyndon Johnson address the nation. Dr. King sat in this chair as President Johnson declared federal protection for Black voters. That support would become the Voting Rights Act of 1965.

Artifact

#### Set of Bricks Used in the Jackson Family Home, Selma, Alabama

Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. and other Civil Rights leaders often stayed in the Selma, Alabama, home of Dr. Sullivan and Richie Jean Sherrod Jackson during the struggle to secure voting rights in the 1960s. King and several leaders once held a meeting on a bed, which collapsed beneath them. The meeting devolved into laughter. These bricks became the support for the repaired bed.

Article

#### Victorian Hairwork Albums

At The Henry Ford, we often think of the countless stories of innovation that our institution can tell through archival materials—from the automotive parts drawings to the Bill Stumpf and Robert Propst design collections.

[](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/victorian-hairwork-albums)

Article

#### Thoughtfully Crafted: Sustainable Products from Liberty Craftworks

At The Henry Ford, we have dedicated teams of craftspeople that create beautiful products in our Pottery, Glass, Weaving, and Print Shops that make up Liberty Craftworks in Greenfield Village. While these artisans use historic techniques, they also do their fair share of innovation. One of the areas in which we have recently expanded is in our offering of products from Liberty Craftworks that utilize recyclable material sourced on-site.

[](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/thoughtfully-crafted-sustainable-products-from-liberty-craftworks)

Article

#### Making the Connection: Phoning on the Go

When telephones were first introduced in the late 1800s, people wanting a phone would subscribe to the local phone company’s services and receive a telephone on lease. Using the telephone at this time meant that both caller and receiver were tethered to their respective locations.

[](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/making-the-connection-phoning-on-the-go)

Article

#### \*Batteries and Fuel Not Included: The Hop Rod, the World’s First “Powerized” Pogo Stick

Over the years many fun - but dangerous - toys have been marketed to children, only to be discontinued or banned. Remember chemistry sets with toxic materials? Or lawn darts capable of serious injury?

[](https://www.thehenryford.org/collections/explore/articles/batteries-and-fuel-not-included-the-hop-rod-the-worlds-first-powerized-pogo-stick)

* Unlock Deeper Access to Our Collections
  
  Research Services
  
  ### Unlock Deeper Access to Our Collections
  
  Whether you're an academic, a researcher, or simply a curious mind, our research services provide exclusive ways to engage with the collections at The Henry Ford. From personalized assistance to hands-on access, our services are designed to help you explore, discover, and utilize our vast resources.
* Be Part of Our Story
  
  Get Involved
  
  ### Be Part of Our Story
  
  Become a part of the living legacy of The Henry Ford by contributing your time, knowledge, or resources. Whether through volunteering, donating, or participating in our special programs, your involvement helps preserve and share the rich history of American innovation and ingenuity.

Discover an Artifact

With one click, discover a randomly selected artifact from The Henry Ford’s digitized collection. You never know what piece of American innovation, ingenuity, or everyday life you’ll encounter next.

Surprise Me
